extract_text: "Quality statement 4: Ongoing training in adrenaline auto-injector use | Anaphylaxis | Quality standards"
“Overview | Anaphylaxis: assessment and referral after emergency treatment | Guidance”

Patients, or carers of patients, who carry Emerade 300 or 500 microgram adrenaline auto-injector pens should immediately contact their GP to obtain a prescription for, and be supplied with two auto-injectors of a different brand.
They should ensure they know how to use the replacement pen, as each brand of pen works differently.

This precautionary recall is because some 300 microgram and 500 microgram Emerade auto-injector pens may rarely fail to activate if they are dropped... Premature activation has also been detected... after they have been dropped.
Patients are reminded to carry two pens with them at all times as normal.

Exposure to high temperature may increase the risk of pen failure... should not be exposed to temperatures above 25°C... If travelling to a hot climate, patients should visit their doctor to obtain a prescription for an alternative brand of adrenaline pen.
If an Emerade pen does need to be used, it should be pressed very firmly against the thigh. If this does not result in activation, the patient should immediately use their second pen.
Training videos on how to use the 0.15mg EpiPen and 0.3mg EpiPen and Jext 150 pen and 300 pen are available on the respective manufacturers’ websites.
BSACI primary care guidance: prescribe two AAIs for those at significant risk and advise patients to always carry them.
BSACI emphasises device-specific training and coordination between specialist and primary care teams to avoid gaps in care.
BSACI notes prescribing can act as a safety net when anaphylaxis risk is uncertain, pending specialist review, and that patients prescribed an AAI/at risk should be referred to an allergy specialist.
"Quality statement 1: Initial education in adrenaline auto-injector use | Anaphylaxis | Quality standards"
"Introduction | Anaphylaxis: assessment and referral after emergency treatment | Guidance"
UK incidence increasing (unquantified in excerpt).
ABCDE approach recommended for recognition/management.
Early intramuscular adrenaline is treatment of choice.
High-risk individuals should carry an AAI and receive training/support.
Treatment varies by location/equipment/skills.
MHRA approved adrenaline (epinephrine) nasal spray (EURneffy) for emergency treatment of anaphylaxis in the UK.
Introduces a nasal spray formulation as a needle-free alternative to auto-injectors.
Intended for adults and children ≥30 kg.
Ready-to-use single dose; delivers entire contents (2 mg) upon activation.
Do not press plunger before inserting into nostril or the single dose will be lost.
Can be used even when the nose is congested due to cold or allergy.
Patients should carry two sprays in case a second dose is needed and tell friends/family for emergencies.
MHRA will keep safety and effectiveness under close review; Yellow Card reporting referenced.
Approved via International Recognition Procedure (IRP) with targeted assessment.

From 1 October 2017, schools in England will be allowed to purchase adrenaline auto-injector (AAI) devices without a prescription, for emergency use on children who are at risk of anaphylaxis but whose own device is not available or not working.
Guidance for schools on creating a policy around the use of emergency auto-injectors (AAI).

Guideline summary emphasizes inclusion of written treatment plans and training recommendations; references dosing/absorption and risk assessment for number of devices required (details not present in excerpt).
UK teen non-use of AAI during anaphylaxis is reported as >80% (n=245) in a cited survey.
Delayed or absent adrenaline is framed as a risk factor for fatal anaphylaxis.
Teen dosing-transition is explicitly discussed: 300 mcg vs 500 mcg in teenagers >40 kg; local guidance suggests considering switching at 40–45 kg.
Underdosing is quantified in the rationale (300 mcg at 40 kg ~30% under-dose by weight).
Needle length differences (15 mm vs 23 mm) are proposed to be assessed for impact on delivery.
Carry 2 in-date AAIs at all times; replace before expiry.
Device-specific training and practise with trainer devices is recommended before an emergency.
Use adrenaline immediately at first signs; do not delay; call 999 even if improving; use second device if not improved after 5 minutes.
Indications include exercise-induced and idiopathic anaphylaxis (relevant to sport settings).
Only one AAI brand is available in 500 micrograms in the UK; dosing is guided by age and bodyweight per SmPC.

Labelled strength may not reflect early systemic exposure; device/patient factors can affect blood levels; PK/PD studies were mandated; past Emerade activation failures/recall and reintroduction after corrective actions; report defects via Yellow Card.
Carry at all times for immediate use before emergency services arrive.
Immediate adrenaline at first signs/suspected anaphylaxis; administer even if in doubt; then dial 999.
ABC (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) recognition: one severe symptom warrants adrenaline.
Second dose at 5 minutes if no improvement; supports two-device availability.
Brand-specific training imperative due to device design differences; trainer devices encouraged.
Posture management (do not stand/sit) is under-recognised; recommend pictograms/videos.
Patients are often reluctant to use or carry injected adrenaline; this can delay treatment and increase risk of severe outcomes.
A compact, needle-free device is likely to reduce reluctance to use/carry adrenaline and should result in more timely treatment (framed as expectation, not proven outcome).
Recommendation for shared decision-making when counselling on EURneffy vs injectable adrenaline.
Maintain Allergy Action Plan and call emergency services (999) after any adrenaline use, regardless of route/device.
Tabled comparison: auto-injectors less compact/not easily carried and involve injection vs EURneffy more compact/easily carried and needle-free, alongside ‘very limited clinical experience’ caveat.
Prompt IM adrenaline is the cornerstone of therapy in hospital and community.
Referral to specialist paediatric allergy clinics for children and young people at risk.
Management plans are recommended and may reduce frequency/severity of further reactions.
Local multidisciplinary networks should link primary/community care, social care, education and hospital practice.
Skin signs may be absent in a significant proportion of anaphylaxis cases.
Under-18 dosing is weight-dependent (10 microgram/kg) and may be 150 or 300 microgram depending on weight (SmPC-referenced).
Explicit weight thresholds (e.g., 15–30 kg vs >30 kg) illustrate the dose-transition point relevant as young people gain weight.
Device instructions vary by brand; prescribing by brand and device-specific training are emphasised.
MHRA-advised behaviours: carry two in-date devices, replace before expiry, and use brand-specific training/trainer devices.
MHRA toolkit resources exist for recognition/response and AAI use education.
ED cohort (Taiwan, adults) quantified low epinephrine use (34.1%) and delayed use (25.2%) with high adjunct medication use (>90% steroids/antihistamines).
Epinephrine administration associated with markers of severe presentation and ambulance arrival, suggesting thresholding and potential delays in recognition/decision-making.
Even with hypotension, epinephrine was given in fewer than half of cases, indicating under-treatment despite severe features.
Discussion reiterates guideline position: early IM epinephrine is cornerstone; antihistamines do not treat airway/hypotension/shock; diagnostic variability can drive underuse.
Mentions noninvasive sublingual epinephrine tablets as ‘easy-to-carry’ community option (contextual, not product-specific).
UK adolescent adherence and carriage are low despite high confidence/knowledge; membership of support groups and having a management plan correlate with better adherence (Jones et al., UK).
In UK multicentre paediatric/teen sample, most anaphylaxis episodes were not treated with the prescribed AAI, commonly due to perceived lack of necessity or uncertainty (Noimark et al., UK).
AYA carriage varies by situation (e.g., lower during sports/tight clothes) and device inconvenience/comfort is a stated barrier (non-UK AYA studies).
Peer dynamics including bullying are reported and plausibly contribute to concealment and non-carrying (non-UK AYA study).
Adrenaline (epinephrine) emphasized as the most important/first-line acute treatment; repeated cautions not to delay IM adrenaline.
Fixed-dose auto-injector strengths noted for lay use (150/300/500 µg) alongside weight-adjusted IM dosing; second dose recommended after 5–10 min if inadequate response.
Clinical criteria for anaphylaxis (multi-system involvement; respiratory/cardiovascular compromise) provided for recognition training.
Risk augmentation described (exercise, stress, alcohol, infection), relevant to real-world teen contexts.
Adolescent-linked clinical signals: bronchial obstruction risk especially in children/adolescents; ‘sense of impending doom’ in adolescents/adults.
Prompt IM adrenaline is first-line; antihistamines/corticosteroids should not delay adrenaline.
Delayed adrenaline is associated with worse outcomes; pre-hospital adrenaline is associated with better outcomes.
Recognition is difficult due to variable presentation (including absence of skin symptoms); mild cases can progress rapidly.
Repeat adrenaline may be needed at 5–15 minute intervals; biphasic reactions require monitoring.
Exercise/asthma/drugs can act as co-factors, increasing severity at lower exposures; mechanisms remain poorly understood.
Schools are common settings for paediatric reactions and can have preparedness/training gaps.
Adolescents/teenagers are described as higher-risk due to risk-taking/avoidance and delayed help-seeking; alcohol noted as a co-factor.
IM epinephrine dosing principle: 0.01 mg/kg up to max initial 0.5 mg.
Fixed-dose EAIs can underdose as patient weight increases (explicit statement above 30 kg).
Consider 0.5 mg EAI at ≥45 kg via shared decision-making (CSACI recommendation).
Early epinephrine (prehospital) associated with lower risk of uncontrolled reactions in a cited paediatric study.
Needle length/IM delivery concerns noted (e.g., may not reach muscle in many women) and device characteristics vary by dose.
Prescriber feedback identified confusion due to different brand recommendations for the 150→300 microgram transition.
Previously, Jext switched at 30 kg while EpiPen recommended 300 micrograms from 25 kg.
BSACI and EAACI guidance supported transition to 300 micrograms at 25 kg.
BNF/BNFC aligned Jext weight ranges with EpiPen so ≥25 kg may receive 300 micrograms.
BNF/BNFC notes dosing via auto-injector may differ from the product licence (unlicensed-use statement).
Implementation timing: Dec 2024 online/in-app update; print inclusion 2025+.
“Reminder system for adrenaline autoinjectors does not work” (title only; no methods/results provided in chunk).
“BSACI resource: Types of Adrenaline Auto Injectors”
“Last updated: 28 May 2025”
“First contributed: 30 August 2022”
Around 3% of the UK population have IgE-mediated food allergy and are at risk of potentially life-threatening allergic reactions.
Food-induced anaphylaxis represented 29.4% of reported anaphylaxis admissions, and increased significantly from 1.23 to 4.02 admissions per 100,000 population per annum over the study period.
Despite an annual increase of 5.7% in hospitalisation for food-induced anaphylaxis between 1998 and 2018, the case fatality rate more than halved (0.7% in 1998 to <0.3% in 2018).
152 deaths were identified during the study period where the cause was likely to have been food-induced anaphylaxis.
At least 86 (46%) fatalities were triggered by peanut or tree nuts.
Cows' milk was reported to be the most common cause of fatal anaphylaxis in children aged under 16 years.
40% of individuals with prior food-induced anaphylaxis were not prescribed AAI and at least 59% did not have AAI on repeat prescription.
Most healthcare visits for food allergy occurred in general practice.
Less than 3% of individuals with a diagnosis of food allergy attended Accident & Emergency during the study period (2008–2018).
NHS states anaphylaxis is life-threatening and rapid onset; advises calling 999 for key symptoms and using an adrenaline auto-injector.
NHS advises carrying 2 adrenaline auto-injectors at all times and using a second auto-injector if not improved after 5 minutes.
NHS advises repeated training each time prescribed, practising with a trainer device, and teaching friends how to use it.
NHS includes practical readiness guidance: avoid extreme temperatures and check expiry dates regularly.
NHS includes an equity note: colour change may be easier to see on palms/soles in brown/black skin.
Device activation problems reported; dose may not be delivered; more force may be needed.
Switching guidance: Emerade 500 microgram users advised to obtain replacement devices (EpiPen/Jext max 300 micrograms).
Regulator statement referencing data supporting 500→300 replacement for a single pen.
Carry two pens at all times; if first does not activate, use second immediately.
Training burden: brands differ in administration; patients urged to review instructions, videos, obtain trainer pens; limited face-to-face training (remote/self-directed options).
Access/logistics steps for replacement prescription and pharmacy supply/home delivery (COVID-era).
Class 1 recall due to potential non-delivery/premature activation; damage may be invisible before use.
Dropped-device scenario highlighted as potential trigger for failure/premature activation.
Training mandated when switching brands because device operation differs; trainer pens encouraged for practice.

Emergency algorithm: treat promptly at first signs (even if in doubt), call 999, carry two, use second immediately if first fails; second dose after ~5 minutes if not improving/deteriorating; attempt reactivation while awaiting ambulance.
School access guidance includes teenagers carrying devices when competent; do not lock devices away.
Supply fragility noted; limits on number of pens per patient with exceptions for school needs.
Dose-transition statement: 300µg alternatives may replace 500µg based on blood-adrenaline (PK) studies.
Cohort: 46 respondents aged 13 to 18 years (median age, 16 years), with 34 (74%) reporting at least one food allergy.
For “I am scared to eat food from a new restaurant,” in the food allergy group: 20% sometimes, 23% often, 33% almost always.
35% of the food allergy group (and 0% of no-food-allergy group) said they often felt scared when eating with an adult other than their parent.
Feeling scared when eating at school or camp: 20% sometimes, 17% often, 23% almost always (food allergy group).
Worry about what they eat while traveling: 37% often and 17% almost always (food allergy group).
“Between March 2019 and March 2023, 54 children and young people in the UK died from asthma.”
“The death rate was four times higher for children from more deprived areas.”
“65% attended an emergency department or had an emergency admission in the year before their death.”
“families living with deprivation are known to struggle to engage and navigate through the NHS.”
“The healthcare system needs to shift towards a more patient-centred, community-based service to better support children with asthma.”
Under-recognition by patients and HCPs contributes to delayed/inadequate treatment.
Epinephrine is first-line, should be given immediately for suspected anaphylaxis, and is safe even if diagnosis uncertain.
Avoid replacing/delaying epinephrine with antihistamines/steroids; sedating antihistamines can confound assessment and add risk.
Correct technique matters (IM anterolateral thigh; upper arm is inferior for absorption).
Repeat dosing may be needed (every 5–15 minutes as necessary).
Biphasic reactions occur in a meaningful minority; observation should be individualized.
Rare ‘no-EMS’ home management requires competence + immediate access to ≥2 in-date, weight-appropriate EAIs.
Cofactors (exercise, alcohol, stress, infection) can modulate severity/threshold—relevant to teen contexts (sport/social situations).
Strong recommendation for immediate emergency call and use of available epinephrine auto-injector per instructions (bystander/caregiver pre-arrival actions).
Epinephrine first-line for Grade II–IV anaphylaxis; administer as soon as possible.
IM epinephrine dosing framework with age threshold (≥14 years max 0.5 mg vs <14 years max 0.3 mg) and repeat dosing interval 5–15 minutes.
Technique: IM injection in mid-anterolateral thigh.
Discharge education/training recommended for patients/caregivers (diagnostic criteria, avoidance, first-line treatments).
Recognition nuance: not all anaphylaxis has skin/mucosal symptoms; onset often within 30–60 minutes; respiratory/cardiovascular compromise indicates severe anaphylaxis.
Observational evidence cited: pre-ED epinephrine associated with reduced admission and shorter ED stay (with caveats).
Device failure/premature activation risk if knocked/dropped; internal damage not easily identifiable.
Carry-two standard reiterated for all patients.
Training/retraining is 'vital' when switching brands due to different instructions.
Secondary statement that 300 microgram EpiPen/Jext may substitute for Emerade 500 based on similar blood adrenaline levels (primary studies not provided).
EpiPen vs IM syringe PK/PD comparison (0.3 mg) in randomized crossover design stratified by STMD.
Early systemic exposure (partial AUCs) was greater/faster with EpiPen vs manual IM syringe across STMD strata.
EpiPen PK reported as similar across STMD groups, including when needle may not reach muscle (no imaging confirmation).
“It is recommended that patients are prescribed two pens which they should carry at all times.”
“Adrenaline auto-injectors: reminder for prescribers to support safe and effective use. See MHRA Drug Safety Update (November 2021)”
“Patients asked to return Emerade 300 and 500 microgram adrenaline pens for replacement. See MHRA Press release (May 2023)”
“EpiPen Jr® ... 150micrograms... Children 7.5-25 kg”
“EpiPen® ... 300micrograms... Adults, and children weighing over 25kg”
“BNF Update - 8th July 2025: The body-weight ranges for Jext® have been aligned with those of EpiPen® so that all children weighing 25kg and over may be given a dose of 300 micrograms.”
MHRA-reiterated recommendation to prescribe and carry two AAIs at all times.
Advice to maintain two devices via repeat prescriptions (logistics burden).
Advice to check expiry and replace in time (maintenance burden).
Mandatory training for every prescription, irrespective of prior use; practice with trainer devices (skills decay/technique risk).
Brand-specific prescribing to support correct device technique and avoid confusion.

The NICE guidelines on food allergies and anaphylaxis provide detailed recommendations for the diagnosis, management, and long-term care of individuals, particularly children and young people, at risk of allergies and anaphylaxis.
They cover key areas such as taking an allergy-focused clinical history, appropriate diagnostic testing, patient education, and the use of adrenaline auto-injectors.
Additionally, the guidelines emphasise the importance of specialist referrals for complex cases and the need for ongoing monitoring to ensure effective management and patient safety.
Allergies can impact almost every aspect of daily life — from school and travel to attending a hospital appointment.
Secondary-school transition described as confusing and requiring preparation and support.
School selection should consider inclusivity and emotional support (Ofsted sections).
Responsibility/knowledge may be unclear at open days; identify a medical-conditions lead/key staff.
After starting secondary school, arrangements should be checked and monitored in practice.
Parents should regularly discuss allergy management with the child to support proactive self-management.
Extracurricular activities/lessons involving allergens require known staff contact pathways.
Four paediatric cases of hooked/embedded EpiPen needles (including school/home/restaurant contexts) requiring ED management/removal.
Proposed mechanisms include movement during injection, striking bone, and off-center firing with device component damage.
Cited evidence that unintentional injections and upside-down use occur, including in trained users; multiple cited statistics from US sources.
Noted cross-country variation in recommended hold time (England cited as 3 seconds; many European countries 10 seconds).
“Allergy Guidelines and Pathways – Children and Young People's Allergy Network Scotland”
“Search this website”
Large UK THIN cohort quantifying steep growth in paediatric AAI prescribing and total devices issued over 2000–2012.
Wide heterogeneity in devices prescribed per child (0–40/year) and multi-device provisioning patterns consistent with multiple settings.
Junior (150 µg) prescribing in older children (≥6) suggests dosing-transition complexity without weight-based confirmation.
Guideline/regulatory context: MHRA recommends carrying a second device; BSACI guidance mentioned as differing on number of devices.
Immediate escalation instruction (dial 999) for suspected anaphylaxis.
Treat if in doubt; avoid waiting for diagnostic certainty.
Recognition caveat: isolated skin rash alone is not an indication for adrenaline.
Age-banded IM adrenaline dosing includes >12 years at 500 micrograms (adult band).
Repeat dosing after 5 minutes if no improvement (max 2 doses/15 minutes).
Auto-injector storage guidance: outer carton; do not store >25°C; not refrigerated.
Mandatory/regular anaphylaxis training and competency requirements for relevant staff.
Protocol recommendations: storage/access to emergency medication (emergency kits), staff training, and emergency response to anaphylaxis.
Operational detail: emergency kits stored in an unlocked, central location so staff can quickly locate medication.
Secondary school relevance: responsible pupils should carry emergency medication on their person; suggested practical storage locations (pencil case/blazer pocket/bag).
Time-to-treatment logic: on-person AAI may be quicker than collecting from central storage in large/split-site schools.
Inclusion/anti-stigma: avoid policies that make allergic pupils stand out; separate tables/queues may isolate and encourage bullying.
Transition settings: visitors/after-school clubs; trips/excursions; transport measures (as cited via EAACI/DoH checklists).
Risk context: a proportion of severe school reactions occur in pupils without known allergy history (statistic presented without visible sourcing in extract).
Teen risk: explicit statement that patients in their teenage years are at increased risk.
Carriage expectation: MHRA advice that all patients prescribed an AAI should carry two at all times.
School mitigation: schools commonly request AAIs stored on-site to avoid forgetting.
Logistics burden: typical prescribing of four AAIs (school set + carried set).
Equity/access: remote living/reduced access to medical help listed as a higher-risk situation.
Risk factors: high-risk food allergy plus asthma increases risk even if prior reactions were mild.
Refractory anaphylaxis defined as non-response after ≥2 doses of ≥300 µg adrenaline.
Refractory cases show markedly higher arrest and death outcomes vs severe non-refractory cases.
Refractory cases are disproportionately perioperative/medical-procedure related, with more IV adrenaline and faster repeat dosing.
Two in three children and one in four adults are afraid of needles. (quoted)
Needle fear/phobia can delay epinephrine administration when delivery is mainly via autoinjectors. (commentary)
Top 15 TikTok videos on autoinjector fears had >5 million likes. (reach)
60% showed needle fear/phobia; 40% had trigger warnings about a large needle. (content characteristics)
None of the creators appeared to be healthcare providers; advocacy interest in posting proper-use videos. (education gap/intervention idea)
Two-device on-person carriage requirement is explicitly stated (including under EURneffy section).
Device-specific technique and ‘prescribe by brand’ emphasised; links to training videos and trainer devices; expiry alert services referenced.
Dose review needed as children grow; BNF weight-band alignment (≥25 kg → 300 micrograms) cited.
Emerade discontinuation noted (SPS Jan 2026 referenced).
EURneffy locally positioned only for confirmed needle phobia.
Anaphylaxis can be rapid, fatal, and may present without skin features (risk of delayed recognition).
Teenager/young adult status is explicitly listed as a relative indication for prescribing an AAI in food allergy even after mild-to-moderate reactions.
Guidance recommends prescribing multiple AAIs to cover different settings (home/emergency bag and nursery/school).
Travel/remote access and exercise-induced anaphylaxis are flagged as higher-risk contexts requiring readiness.
Weight/size-related dosing notes imply transition points where dosing/device selection may need review.
UK prevalence estimates for food allergy (children and adults).
Primary-care knowledge/skills gap signal (self-perceived adequacy).
Clear emergency response recommendation: IM adrenaline + immediate ambulance transfer to ED.
Prescribe two AAIs and provide training pending specialist review.
Ensure written management plans and communicate changes to schools.
High-risk features include food allergy with unstable/moderate-to-severe asthma; cofactor/exercise-related reactions noted.
Carry two AAIs on-person at all times (UK formulary statement).
Children’s AAI doses change over time; review dosing (transition relevance).
BNF (8 July 2025) alignment: ≥25 kg can receive 300 micrograms (Jext aligned with EpiPen).
Technique is device-specific; prescribe by brand to match training.
Signposting to training devices/videos and expiry alert services.
Local formulary positions EURneffy only for confirmed needle phobia.
"Epinephrine auto-injector use in adolescents at risk of anaphylaxis: A qualitative study in Scotland, UK."
"What factors affect the carriage of epinephrine auto-injectors by teenagers?"
"How do teenagers manage their food allergies?"
"The use of adrenaline autoinjectors by children and teenagers."
"Risk-taking and coping strategies of adolescents and young adults with food allergy."
"Fatal anaphylaxis in the UK, 1992-2001."
"Epinephrine in anaphylaxis: Preclinical study of pharmacokinetics after sublingual administration of taste-masked tablets for potential pediatric use"
Anaphylaxis definition (acute onset; skin/mucosa plus respiratory/cardiovascular compromise).
Teach recognition and appropriate response, including calling 999 for severe symptoms.
Avoidance advice includes label reading, asking ingredients, informing nursery/school/other carers; caution with parties/buffets.
Prescribe an AAI if probable allergy with history suggesting anaphylaxis, or if regular asthma preventer medication is used.
Two AAIs should be available at all times; nursery/school requires two (citing MHRA guidance).
Training must be device-specific; use videos and trainer pens.
Local preference: EpiPen is preferred device in GGC.
Testing/referral guidance cautions against indiscriminate IgE testing absent reaction history.
England NHS prescription-data study (Clinical and Experimental Allergy) assessing non-standard AAI dosing in primary care; large cohort of latest 150 microgram prescriptions.
Underdosing risk inferred via weight centile estimates; age-based switching to 300 microgram at ~6 years implies high potential mis-dosing proportion.
Deprivation gradient in non-standard prescribing noted, suggesting inequality in prescribing quality.
Economic modelling (Archives of Disease in Childhood) suggests ‘spare AAI in schools’ is cost-effective and can reduce multi-set/expiry waste burden.
Skin and/or mucosal changes (flushing, urticaria, angioedema) can also occur, but are absent in 20% of cases.
Identify any potential triggers (e.g., foods, drugs, stings, exercise) in the 4 hours before the reaction if at all possible.
Food is the most common trigger for anaphylaxis in children. Foods most associated are: peanuts, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, cow's milk, soya and egg.
Prescribe up to 4 self-injectable adrenaline devices (2 accompanying the patient at all times, and 2 at school) with appropriate training...
All patients who have been prescribed an adrenaline autoinjector whether in primary or secondary care should have written guidance on how to use them.
After emergency treatment for suspected anaphylaxis in Devon, the paediatric medical team should offer people a referral to a specialist allergy service (age-appropriate where possible).
Referrals may need to be completed by primary care if a child or young person has received emergency treatment away from their local hospital.
Acute management of anaphylaxis should be treated according to Resuscitation Council guidelines.
Please follow the link here to BSACI allergy action plan.
Fatal food-induced anaphylaxis peaks in 2nd–3rd decades (teen/young adult risk signal).
Delayed/no epinephrine is repeatedly linked to fatal outcomes; reasons include recognition/knowledge deficits, technique errors, lack of access, and non-use despite availability.
Out-of-home settings (school/restaurant/party/trips) feature prominently in described fatal events; Italian paediatric/teen cases often occurred outside home with limited AAI availability.
UK data cited: trigger patterns (peanut/tree nuts; cow’s milk prominent in school-age deaths) and registry finding that a substantial minority of deaths occurred despite timely adrenaline.
BSACI SOCC has developed Allergy Action Plans for adults and children at risk of anaphylaxis.
Plans developed with UK patient charities Allergy UK and Anaphylaxis UK.
Guidelines/slides/posters are available for healthcare providers expected to deal with anaphylaxis (UK responder-facing resource signpost).
Separate vaccination-setting anaphylaxis guidance is signposted (setting-specific protocol context).
Anaphylaxis e-learning exists for expected responders; certificate valid for two years (refresher cadence context).
Baseline readiness gaps: 3.3% previously trained; many schools lacked action plans and on-site emergency medication (self-reported).
A single 60-minute training with AAI demonstration/practice significantly improved teacher self-efficacy for recognizing anaphylaxis and administering adrenaline.
Quasi-experimental pilot simulation in trainee teachers comparing IM autoinjector trainer (Jext 300 µg) vs intranasal device simulator using an ERC-guideline-based checklist.
Large pre-training knowledge/confidence gaps: most did not know appropriate treatment; few had prior anaphylaxis training.
Critical response-step failure: substantial proportion did not call emergency services in scenario.
Autoinjector step errors common (hold time, massage), supporting technique/training gap narrative in lay responders.
Intranasal device simulator achieved higher checklist compliance and faster administration time; key failure mode was incomplete dose delivery, supporting need for training even with simpler devices.
For treatment of anaphylaxis adrenaline is given IM in a dose of 500 micrograms (0.5mL adrenaline injection 1 in 1,000).
At SFH stocked on all wards (in the normal IV drugs cupboard).
Pre-hospital vs criteria mismatch: high proportion meeting anaphylaxis criteria but low adrenaline administration before ED arrival (paediatric 43%; adult 36%).
ED under-treatment: only ~56–57% meeting criteria on arrival received adrenaline in ED.
Treatment delays quantified (paediatric median 20 minutes to adrenaline).
Discharge preparedness gaps: substantial minority left without AAI despite being assessed as needing one; written action plans provided to ~1/3.
Route/technique signal in older paediatrics (≥10y): nebulised adrenaline used instead of IM, suggesting hesitancy/confusion.
UK schools can legally stock spare AAIs (since 2017) as a backup alongside pupils’ prescribed devices.
Spare AAIs may be used when a prescribed pupil’s own AAI is unavailable (e.g., broken, expired, inaccessible, misfired).
Non-HCP school staff may administer AAIs if competent/trained; device brand differences require familiarity/training.
Age-based dosing guidance for school stock includes teenagers (12+) using 300 micrograms; age-based criteria are positioned as reducing emergency confusion from multiple doses/devices.
Operational pathway notes authorisation requirements and calling emergency services (999) when appropriate to authorise use.

"publishing new insights on ... the cost of food hypersensitivities (which found that on average an FHS household spends an additional 12-27% more on weekly food purchases compared to households without FHS)."
"Kitchen Life 2... uses motion sensitive cameras... over 650 hours of footage... almost 300,000 data labels"
“The top 5 science landing pages… 2. Food hypersensitivity – 2,928 page visitors”
Available as: Adults: EpiPen® Auto-injector 0.3mg or Jext® 300microgram injection. Children: EpiPen® Jr Auto-injector 0.15mg or Jext® 150microgram auto-injector.

Primary school-aged children who are unable to carry their own pens at school require two pens for school and two for other times. Parents/carers may require up to four pens to be renewed if pens have expired.
This should be done by issuing two prescriptions for two pens each.
Pharmacies can order a maximum of 2 autopen (0.15mg or 0.3mg) devices at a time against a valid prescription
Injection technique is device specific. To ensure patients receive the auto-injector device that they have been trained to use, prescribers should prescribe by brand.
Emerade® 150microgram, 300microgram and 500microgram pre-filled devices have been discontinued following a long term supply issue.
For treatment of anaphylaxis adrenaline is given IM in a dose of 500 micrograms (0.5mL adrenaline injection 1 in 1,000). N.B. dosing differs for self administration - see above
Adrenaline (epinephrine) nasal spray (EURneffy®) — Non Formulary — Grey no formal assessment
Adolescence described as a high-risk life stage due to reduced parental supervision, increased peer time, lower risk perception, and inconsistent management behaviours.
Adolescents report knowledge/skills gaps: triggers, recognising anaphylaxis, and knowing when/how to use AAIs.
Belonging/peer context: desire to manage safely without feeling like an inconvenience.
Transition tasks (e.g., speaking to restaurant staff) are challenging, indicating community-setting risk management barriers.
Low confidence in school/peer settings; concerns about others’ ability to manage an emergency; delays in emergency treatment linked to these gaps.
AAI carriage is recommended for those at risk of anaphylaxis; plans should detail how to administer medications.
EMA/MHRA recommend access to two devices.
Training should be structured, comprehensive, device-specific, repeated at follow-up, and tailored to age/language/setting.
Risk-management advice should cover school/college, restaurants, travel/relocation, and cofactors (exercise, alcohol, illness, drugs).
Relative indications include ‘Teenager or young adult’ and ‘Remote from medical help or prolonged travel abroad’.
Even with immunotherapy/omalizumab, continued carriage of rescue meds and an action plan is required.
It is advisable to prescribe adrenaline auto-injector by brand to avoid confusion.
